transcript.transcript: And there is a red dot. So we should be recording. And. Microphone. Okay. Speakers. I think we are good. Oh yes. It's saying right there that it's recording. Okay. All right. My name is Maggie Bude. And I am in the Junior League of Boise. Today is March 16th. 2026 and with Lindy High Talk about the history of junior league and women's lives in Idaho. This oral history project is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society and America. 250 in Idaho commemoration, the oral history project of which this interview is a part, supports the commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty and justice through meaningful community engagement, and recognize, appreciate and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Lindy, thanks for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences through these questions. I hope to gain insights into women's lives in Idaho and especially Junior League with some general questions, and we can get into other specifics, uh, as we go along. Um, so before we jump into questions, can you state and spell your name? My real name or the name I go by? Um, well, how about both? Um, my name is Lindy, L I n d y. Hi, H I g h, and some of my legal documents are in the name Linda. My birth certificate is in the name Linda Lee. Okay. Have you always been Lindy? Okay, so I know you've told me you're a fourth generation idahoan. Fifth? Fifth generation idahoan. All right. Um, what do you know of how your family came to Idaho? Oh, um, my great, great parents were living in Montana with their children, so my great great grandmother's daughter by her first marriage, who was pregnant and waiting for her husband to come back. And, um, the my great great grandfather decided the family needed to return to Seattle because the conditions were so bad in Montana. So. I remember telling her one night she shouldn't go out so much. She should stay home with us more. And why was she doing all this stuff? And she said, if you live in a community, you need to make the community better. And that always stuck with me over the years. My mother was one of the most talented people I've ever known and one of the smartest and she could do. I swear she could do anything. She rewired appliances. She painted the house. She laid tiles. She knit, she crocheted. She did Hardanger. She did ceramics. She was a wonderful cook. I could go on and on. But the only thing she ever did that she couldn't do was she tried her hand at stained glass. And that didn't work for her. But she's the one who really encouraged us to do community things. Um, how did you decide to enter journalism as a career? Uh, I graduated from college and went to work for the Idaho Historical Society, working on their federal records collection. And then I left there to have a baby and, uh, was looking for work in the Public relations director for the College of Idaho, knew that United Press International was looking for another reporter, and she recommended that, um, that they interview me. I had no idea what a wire service did, but I always got. But but I always got good grades in English and, and the important part of it. Okay, I'll be honest, the important part is that the bureau chief kept asking me if I liked sports, and I kept saying no because I wanted to be truthful. And I got hired because it turned out the World Series was going to be on the next month, and all the men in the office wanted to go to the bar, which is no longer in downtown, and watch the World Series, and they wanted to make sure somebody was back in the bureau to answer the phone, and that was me. So the fact that I didn't like sports turned out to be the plus that got me the job, and I knew nothing about what I was doing. I worked on the college paper, but that's all different, And I got lucky because the bureau chief that I worked for taught me how to write. Taught me about state government. Taught me how to listen. Taught me how to pick out what was important for a lead. Everything I did later in life, everything I can trace back to that time I spent at United Press International. And United Press International is still around today. But it's definitely, you know, maybe not as as, as big as it was. Yeah. I don't think it has a US presence anymore. I think it's mostly radio and I think it's mostly international. Not much in the US back then. What what what did it do? Like could you tell us like, what is a wire service? There are two kinds of wire services. One is Associated Press and that's a membership organization where, um, people would, would, uh, pay to be a member of the Associated Press, and then they would get their stories on the wires. I guess I don't need to explain that. It's like a teletype system that brought the stories to you. This was all in Pre-computer days, pre-cell phone days. Uh, United Press International was the younger of the two wire services, and it was a purchase type arrangement so that at the end of a year, if the costs of collecting the news had gone over what was anticipated, everybody who participated in that was levied a certain amount. So if there was a war that year and it was going to cost more to, people had to come up with more money. So there were two different approaches. Um, and we called Associated Press granny because it was older than we were. And UPI was always considered the kind of young upstart, uh. You know, get them, get them by any means you have to do. And the people I worked with, the the other correspondent in the bureau chief were real old timers. And they had wonderful stories about stuffing cloth into, um, payphones so that none of the other reporters could use them and, and answering a phone, a car phone, and hiding under the dashboard so that no other reporter would see the that there was a phone available to use. Anyway, I grew up on that kind of stuff and it was a wonderful experience for me. Um, earlier in your career, as you were kind of learning the ropes, could you remember any significant stories that helped you kind of learn the job? Well, um, one of the old timers said to me, you're really not a full fledged reporter until I and covered the legislature. What was covered? A plane crash and a killing. And I did all those with my first year because Boise is very small. And our bureau chief, um, his theory was send your least experienced person to the scene because the person left in the bureau could coordinate all that inflow of information and write coherent story. So I got an awful lot of experience that I wouldn't have had otherwise. My first big story was a bank robbery up on Vista. And I got up there late because I'd missed a message about it. And all the other reporters were kind of standing to the side. But I didn't let that stand in the way. I walked right to the bank and knocked on the door and held up my little press pass. And the reporters over here were just laughing at me because they'd all been told to move back. And we weren't. The FBI just said, we're not done here yet. So I was too afraid to go back to the bureau and tell my bureau chief that I didn't have any. Information I was. I was more afraid of him than I was of anybody. So I went to across the street to um well, it was a Denny's back in those days and got a Coke and I was watching the bank. And after a couple of hours these women walked out of it. So I went over to one and said, um, excuse me, you probably don't want to talk to me, but if you did, would you tell me what it was like inside? And this woman said, oh, honey. Oh, honey, he put the gun right here in my side. Oh, honey, it was the worst thing. It scared me to death. And then. And she just gave me everything, told me everything. Had a bank robbery went down. I had the best story for him. At that time. Were there a lot of women in this field, or were you one of you? There were not very many women. There was, um, Alice Dieter for channel two and for the observer, which was an independent newspaper at the time. And Sandra Haussegger was at the Statesman. And Mindy Cameron was a reporter for the state. It was a different time. I believe that Alice, Dieter and I were the only two women in one year who were accredited to cover the legislature. And in those days, and I covered the Senate. And in those days, there wasn't even a call of the Senate is when they want to round everybody up to vote. Everybody has to be in the chambers in order to have a vote. And when that happens, it's very formal. They actually lock the doors of the Senate so nobody can get in or out. And the senators are located, they're led in, and then the doors are locked behind them. And if you're in the chambers, you're not allowed to leave. And in those days, there was no bathroom in the Senate for women. There was one for men, but there wasn't one for women. So finally, Edith Miller Klein was a state senator. Boise, and she got up high enough in seniority that I think she was able to talk building services into remodeling and adding a women's restroom. But if you had to go to the bathroom during a call as a Senate, you were just totally out of luck. Do you remember about what year that would have been? Oh yeah. I worked at UPI from 1968 to 1972. And I was there when the Equal Rights Amendment passed. And there was no discussion, really. Um, Edith Miller Klein again got up. I'd never heard of the era. She got up and she described what it was and just very general terms. And then the senators began voting, and one by one, they voted no. And I was astonished, especially when Mike Mitchell from Lewiston voted no because he had voted not to sterilize the insane. He had voted for Equal rights disabled, and he was going to vote against this. And that's when I started thinking, this isn't real. And sure enough, one by one, all the senators then changed their votes. I think there was one vote that stayed no. But all the senators got up and said, I changed my vote from no to I, and it passed. And there was no controversy, no discussion. None of that started until. Well, in the next couple of years. And I was also there the day that they voted to rescind it. I was holding a seat for somebody who'd gone to the bathroom. You got to see a lot of history made over there, a lot of fun. What do you think changed between them passing it and rescinding it? Oh, the Eagle Forum and Phyllis Schlafly and all the terrible things. Why? You might have to share a bathroom with a man. Women might be drafted to go to war. Um, which was ironic because where I worked at the University of Idaho, there were men and women in the office in one bathroom, and somehow we all survived. But there was just a lot of right wing backlash, backlash against it. At that time. And I think people probably, you know, read newspapers more often than they do today. Um, newspapers were a bigger part of public life in the past. Do you feel like you were known in the community or you had a reputation or were a part of public life because you worked in the newspaper? Um, I didn't work for the newspaper at that time. Um, no, not particularly. I was well known in state government because people in state government actually would look at bylines and and pay attention to the news. Really? It was more later. Um, I'd have to go upstairs and look. But sometime in the 80s. Uh, I was asked to write a weekly newspaper column for the statesman, an editorial page column. And, uh, that more than anything, I'd run into a lot of people who'd say, you really sound familiar to me. And I'd ask them, do you read The Statesman? Yes. Well, that's why you see me every week. And, um, so it was really more the than working for United Press, you know, press. Great. Great to be known in state government. But that was about it. Did you ever have a time when you felt you influenced the public or public policy? No. One thing that I read at some point you. And you'll have to correct me if I'm. If I get this wrong. But when the state pen was still used to house prisoners that you actually visited and reported on it at that time. Mhm. Um, what do you recall about the prison in those years? Um, the old pen was the penitentiary, and Dave and I were getting ready to go on, on a vacation, I think. And Marshall, our son was, would have been about. It was in 71, I think. I think it would have been was he's two years old. And, uh, there was a bulletin on on the news we were getting ready to leave, and I just took a handed Marshall to Dave and took the car keys and said, I'll be back when I can. So I drove out to the old pen, to the penitentiary and parked and I could hear shots going off, but I didn't recognize them as shots because they didn't sound like they do in the movies. They sounded more like pop guns. And that surprised me and I wasn't sure that I could get in. And then Alice, Dieter and her husband les pulled up and Alice and I went in with les. They were going to let a man in there, but, um, they let us in. And Jay Schellen from Associated Press, my direct rival, was already there and we went out on one of the up on the second floor, looking out over the yard in the prison where the inmates were milling around. It was a really hot night and nobody wanted to go back in their cells, and there wasn't any air conditioning, and they just said, no, we're not going to do it. And so, um, Jay and I were in this balcony kind of thing, and somebody yelled up and said, get that dame off the balcony. And I grabbed the the grabbed the edge of it and said, up, I will leave the balcony when AP has to leave the balcony. And because I'd heard all these stories from my boss over the years about skullduggery and reporting, the warden said he was going to have a news conference. So I went down to where the news conference was and there was a phone there and I picked up the phone. It was an old dial phone, and I dialed the office, and my boss was at the office because he believed that the most experienced person should be there. And I said, I don't have anything right now, but the warden's going to have a news conference, so I'm just going to put the phone under my shoulder. And so I took notes and then I started transcribing. I started dictating a story to him about what the warden had said, and Jay went off to find another phone. Well, there were no other phones. That was the only phone. So Jay came back and I whispered to my boss, this is the only phone in the prison that has an outside line. And so immediately my boss slowed down and he started saying things like, should that be a period or a comma? Uh, just really take it. Let me read that back to you and see if that's the way you think it should be. And Jay was getting madder and madder. I could just see it. And finally I said into the phone, chanoch. That was the guy's name. Chani. He's going to she's going to come and get me and said, he won't hit you. You're a girl. But eventually, after I was content with my story and I knew we had a lead on Jay, um, I gave him the phone and let him call his story in, but there was a follow up to this that is not part of the, well, really two follow ups. The easy one. I'll tell them in order. A couple of weeks later, Chanak said to me, where are you going to be on Saturday night? I said, we're going to be at home or playing bridge with friends. And he said, well, I want to make sure because I heard something might happen at the prison Wednesday night or next weekend. And I said, you know, if you get hit by a car, who told you? And he said, I can't tell you. It's just a source. I said, if you ever get hit by a car, we're going to lose all these sources. You're the only one who knows who they are. But anyway, I told him that's where I'd be. Well, coincidentally, our bridge player friends were Chuck and Jane Brumbach. Chuck at that time was a deputy attorney general and his state client was the prison. In about 1030 at night the phone rang and in the kitchen I picked it up and it was the warden of the prison. And he said he understood Chuck was at our house and could he speak to him. So I went out and told Chuck to take the phone call. And then This. This does not make me look good. I went into the bedroom and I picked up the extension and I listened to the conversation. They had found the body of a Boise. They found a body in the gym between two mattresses and the guy had been killed. And the warden said who he was. And then just to make sure Chuck understood, he said, this was the guy who killed the Boise State University co-ed. It was a really big deal at the time. And, um, but at the end of it, the warden said, but we're not going to tell any reporters that this happened. So I put the phone down and I went out and I said to Jane, please tell Chuck I'm sorry, but it's my job. And I got in my car and went down to the office and looked up the background on this guy and filed a story that he'd been killed out at the prison. It was years before Chuck spoke to me again. He was. And, you know, it was. That was a pretty skullduggery thing to do. But that was up. His reputation really was scrappy. Scrappy. We were scrappy. Do you ever find out how your bureau chief had advance warning of the story? Nope. He never told me. So months later, a guy that I knew who had worked for the statesman but had a bad habit of writing bad checks, was out at the prison, and he, um. He called me and said, how would you feel about interviewing some inmates who were involved in that prison doing kind of a retrospective on what happened? I said, great. So he said, I'll get about six, six inmates here. The warden thinks it's a good idea. So he gave me the name. He finally told me who I'd be interviewing, and one of them was a guy I know, a meandering, but one of them was a guy who had robbed a 7-Eleven in Boise. His name was Paul Hatton, and the clerk at the store was named Christopher Hatton. They were not related to each other And Christopher Hatton, I believe, was young, 21, 20, something like that. And Paul Hatton took him into the back room and shot him. Killed him. He didn't kill him. He died on the operating table. He shot him in the head. And so one of the people who I was supposed to interview was Paul Hatton. And I said, no, I'm not. I don't want him. I don't want him in this group because it's a privilege for them to get the day off, to talk to a reporter. We were going to have lunch in a private dining room and, you know, it was a change from their normal day to day stuff. It was a big deal to them. I don't want him to have that big deal. So they put another guy in there so good looking and his clothes were just crisp. He just was Gordon. I don't remember his name now. It turned out at the end I said to after they all left, I said to the to the warden, now that Gordon is really a neat guy, what's he in for? And he said, oh, he's in for first degree murder for robbing a gas station and killing the clerk. So yeah, so much for my not giving special privileges. Boy, it was a great job. It was the best job I ever had. I loved that job. I loved reporters are so much fun to be around because they're really cynical and, and they also usually have a lot of inside information. And at least that's the way it was in those days when I went to work for United Press. There was an afternoon paper here in Boise called the Cap Capital, I think, and it folded about two weeks after I went to work for UPI. But people did read newspapers every morning. That was a big deal to get the statesman in. If you were in the statesman and AP and UPI were in direct competition, and if I took play, I was happy all day about it. Now I think maybe this isn't the right time to ask this question, because I was going to ask you about teaching journalism, too. But with the outbreak of Watergate and Woodward and Bernstein now, that really made journalism glamorous in a way. Yeah. And I mean, I think that that has even lasted until today. I mean, I know when I went to college in the early 2000, then people who were journalism majors were still. Yeah, they had all read Woodward and Bernstein and they admired them. And so what was what was it like for that to unfold in real life in real time? Um, the journalism school at the University of Idaho was looking for somebody who had practical experience as opposed to academic experience. And I fit that perfectly. And they all wanted to be Woodward and Bernstein, all of them. And, you know, I thought my mantra was to say, if you can't get the Blackfoot swimming pool bond election right, you're never going to have a chance to be Woodward and Bernstein. So we spent a lot of time talking about, um, how to get things right and how to decide what the good lead was and how to ferret out what was important about it. And I had them cover things around campus so that, um, Elliot Richardson, who was attorney general of the United States, came to speak there and I had them all, um, go cover that. And since I'd been there, I knew what the story was. So, you know, I could sort of criticize what they were doing. And, you know, in terms of how a real reporter would have done it, and they wanted to argue with me about some things. And I said, it's like, I'm your editor. You're going to go to work for a newspaper. Your editor is king. Um, and for example, uh, the statesman never used the term miss M s it would only use Mr. or miss or Mrs.. Um, I'm not sure. Yeah. The New York Times does it now, but it didn't back in those days. And I said I'd like to complain about that, but if my editor says we're not going to do it, we're not going to do it. And in this class, I am your editor. You may not like my rules, but that's the way it is. It was a lot of fun to teach journalism, and I had a lot of really neat students. And one of them is, do you ever see Randy staples? He writes a weekly column for the Press Tribune. He was one of my students. He's written several books. So how many. Why don't you did you start at U of I in 1974? Teaching. Yes, 74 and 75. When we left Boise, I think I started crying. North of McCall, I just I did not want to leave u p I and I had a job up there. I was assistant director of the news bureau at the university. Um, but it turned out that, uh, because I was working for the university, I could get my master's in history and it didn't cost me anything. So not only did I get some interesting experience teaching and a chance to work out a conciliation agreement with the Human Rights Commission, but I got a master's too. So all in all, it was a really good time for me. Will you tell me more about the Human Rights Commission and your involvement in it? There was a group of us called the Women's Caucus, and we, uh. Gosh, it was I mean, I was trying to think back who the members were. They were mostly of the dean of students was one of the, the woman who was the dean of students and the assistant dean of students, and that there was a PE professor and a man from the Art Bureau. Those of us from the news Bureau were in it. Just an assortment of women across campus. And, um, we filed a complaint with the Human Rights Commission saying that women were not treated equally at the University of Idaho. They were not paid equally. Even if the consequence of their decisions was the same as their male counterparts, they were not paid the same. And and because that's in those days, that's how we really measured the importance of your job. What would what was the consequence of your error? If it only affected your department? Yeah. Okay. If it affected the university, that's where it's been affected. Public policy. That was worse. You know, that sort of stuff. And, um, we filed this complaint and we wanted a women's center. We wanted an affirmative action officer. We wanted a. um, classes in women's studies. We wanted a female doctor in a health center. I can't remember now what all the things we wanted were. And so the Human Rights Commission sent its director at the time, a guy named Fred Graham, up to the up to, um, Moscow, and he kind of interviewed everybody, all involved. And then he began negotiating for us. He'd go back and forth, here's the offer. What would you think of this? And eventually we got it. It's called a consolidated agreement where both parties could agree that those changes would be made. And I typed up the final agreement in my kitchen on my little Selectric typewriter, because we didn't have word processors in those days. And, um, three of us signed it. I've got copies of it upstairs, but I know it was the dean of students and me, and there was a third one and I can't remember who it was. And last year was its 50th anniversary, and there were three of us still around who had been involved in that women's caucus. And we were called the godmothers of the Women's Center, and they apparently had a really nice reception. I didn't go up for it. I was busy doing something else. But, um, they were very nice about remembering the role that we had played and that it was important to the university. And the Women's Center is still there, and I'm sure they still have it. Oh, they closed. Right after that. Yeah, it's d e I. Oh I. See. Hey, you know, you wouldn't want you wouldn't want university to have a place for women to meet. My gosh. Okay. Yeah. So they closed it because funding went away or. Uh, I think it was a campus decision. I don't think that it was funded by anything other than university funds. I just think they decided it wasn't needed anymore. And that may be true. I haven't been up to the University of Idaho campus for 30 years, so I don't know. Um, you then you transitioned to work for the state Board of Education back here in Boise. Um, and did you start out working in public information there that was. Hired to be their public information officer? And so you're kind of on the other side of journalism. So going from the offense to the defense. Mhm. So why switch teams? Well, up.I didn't have an opening for me at the time. They had a part time opening, but not a full time opening. And I needed to work full time. We needed the money. And, um, you know, I have to say that was okay because at UPI, I could end up working. You know, Christmas and birthdays and, you know, we're getting the 6:00 shift. And at least when you work for state government, it's usually an eight hour day and you get holidays and weekends off. So that was okay with me. And I'd always thought the guy who had been the State Board of Education's public information officer was a guy named Don Watkins. He was called the godfather of the press here in Boise. And, um, he was going off to be Senator Frank church's press secretary, so he actually recommended me. This is pretty funny. I knew the guy who was the executive director of the State Board of Education, because I had covered him when I was a reporter, and Don called me and said, I'm leaving this job, and I recommended that you take my place, because I'd always thought such a neat job that he had. And, um, He said, listen, I know what's in the budget. And so I can tell you, don't take anything less than 12,000 a year. I was making 8000 a year at the university, so 12,000 a year. Wow. That was that was going to be great. That's what Dave was getting as a starting deputy AG too. So our income was really going to go up and um, they said don't take anything less than 12 because I know he can pay you that. So Milt called me. Milt Small was the director and he kind of interviewed me, but not really. I mean, you know, he'd known me. And then, um, he said, do you have any questions? And I said, yes. Shoot. And I said, how much does the job pay? And he said, I'm thinking 15,000. And then he didn't say anything. And he said, do you have any more questions? And I said, nope, I can be bought. And he was a great guy to work for. He. He was an old historian and knew a lot about state government, and knew a lot about managing people and knew about managing agencies. And I learned a lot from. I have liked everybody that I worked for, everybody I liked and admired them. I never had a bad person that I worked for. Never a bad boss. Never a bad boss. I wish I could say that, but. It makes such a difference, especially if you trust your boss to. Yeah. Yeah. Can you tell me a little bit about kind of just what the work was like? What was the job when you worked for public in public information? Um, I did a lot of internal editing, a lot of internal writing when they needed to report something. Um, at that time, the Administrative Procedure Act was just in its infancy, a new way of making rules. And so I mastered those first steps and I did that for the agency. But mainly I would help organize materials for a board meeting in ways that I knew the press could use them. So send out notices to the press on many questions for things that were not clear to them. And my number one inviolate rule was, do not lie to reporters. Just don't do it. You're going to get found out. And besides that, a good liar needs a great memory. But I've been on the other side, so of course I knew what would make a reporter's coverage much easier. And, um, AP. And UPI didn't always make the meeting. So when that happened, I'd string for them. I'd write the story and call it in and dictate it to them. And they would just accept it. Yeah. Have a good reputation. Yeah. Who is Baby Ashley? Oh, no. That was health and welfare. Okay, so I left the state Board of Education and. And Governor Evans had appointed Rose Bowman to be, uh, director of the Department of Health and Welfare. And Rose told me she wanted to hire me. It was the only classified job I ever had. And, uh, she said all I had to do was finish in the top three or top five of the candidates, and it was in the bag for me. So I took the test, which was really simple. It was a fax. And then I had to write a news release based on those facts. I had to write a speech with a specific audience that was made clear during the course of the speech and had to write something else. I'd taken my office Selectric over to do the test because it was so much easier. Some smart aleck guy said, well, I see that you're a state employee and you brought your typewriter to use. And I would like to use a typewriter because I'm a taxpayer. I would like to use that typewriter. And I said, okay, I'm done anyway and pass it over to him. And I think that disconcerted him so much that because I was really fat. Um, I think that surprised him. So maybe Ashley was an infant who was found in a garbage. Uh, it's, you know, it's it's garbage. It's where you pull down your hand towels, you know, and then you put them in the wastebasket underneath. Okay. That's where the baby was found in the nursing department at Boise State University. And it was taken to Saint Luke's right away. And everybody was interested in baby Ashley. You had, you know, people who immediately wanted to adopt her. And, you know, she was a perfect child. She was quite premature, but she was going to be a perfect child. And everybody wanted her and everybody was going to take charge of her. And there was a little kind of, I won't say fight, but, you know, health and welfare really had responsibility for the for the child. But the hospital had the child possession, I guess is 9/10 of the law even when babies are concerned. And then they did more tests on baby Ashley and baby Ashley didn't have brain. Um, I hydrocephalic I believe is the term had a brain stem that allowed her to breathe. And immediately it changed immediately. Health and welfare was responsible for this child. You know, if anybody was going to pull the pull the plug. Well, that was the funny part. So the head of the hospital and the region four director of Health and Welfare agreed to hold a joint news conference during which the guy from Health and Welfare would say, the baby is in our care and custody right now and we will be making all medical decisions for it. That was kind of fine with the hospital's director. They didn't want anything to do much with it anymore. And so it's the head of the hospital and the head of region four and the actual attending physician, a woman who was the attending physician. Until some reporter says, well, somebody has to make a decision about who's going to pull the plug, who makes that decision. And that as as was worked out, the health and welfare guy said, I will be the one making that decision. This doctor pipes up and says, no, I will be the one making that decision. That baby is my patient and I'll be. And I'm sitting next to the public information officer for the hospital and he's going, no, no. So baby Ashley lived until she would have been born, and then she died. And I guess there would have been a lot of limits to the care that they could have provided. Yeah. She would have never survived. Well, they don't really survive. This couple flew from Connecticut with their six year old hydrocephalic because this woman's carrying this. I mean, the six year old child isn't small, and it couldn't sit on her hip like, you know, that gives you a little support. So she's carrying this child like this who is hydrocephalic. And and she said, see, he has a nice quality of life. He probably did. but didn't know that he existed. And what happens to those children is failure to thrive because they don't know they're alive. And essentially, that's what happened with Ashley. Just failure to thrive plus awful prematurity, which even in a normal child would have been difficult in those days. And health and welfare tried its best to find the mother, went through all the nurses in the nursing program thinking, you know, that was a lie. Nobody seemed to qualify and then pulled all the welfare recipients in the area, pulled their records pregnant at the time, and checked on them. None of them had never found out. I was so sure we would find out. Somebody would talk. Somebody would say, I think that's my cousin, you know, or something. Never. Never knew. So they've never found. Never her family. Um. So I'd like to talk to you. Switch a little gear, because we've gotten into, I guess the later 70s and Junior League came calling in 1977. If I have that right. Is that I can't remember. Um, I think it was around 1977. I think so too. What do you remember about joining at that time? Well, the date in those days it was called semi-secret. I didn't have to go through the secretive, you know, where you got invited to parties by people you've never heard of. And you were being observed the whole time. I didn't do that. So these friends called and asked me to lunch. And it was Rose Bowman, who had been a Democratic candidate for Congress, and Hope Keating, who'd been a Republican candidate for lieutenant governor, and Betty Lou Donnelly, who owned an advertising business here in Boise. I think those were the ones. And they want to take me. And they took me up to, um, Crane Creek Country Club and we ordered drinks. And then one of them finally said to me, Lindy, what do the words Junior League mean to you? And I said, it's an adjective. And she said, what? I said, oh no, junior league type. And there was this long silence and I thought, uh oh, something something went wrong here. And then they pulled out this nice blue folder and they were inviting me to join the Junior League. Did you know that they were members of the Junior League? No, I didn't know anything about Junior League. I mean, other than what I'd read in novels. I didn't know anything. And when I joined the women's, the. You wore name tags and they said, like Mrs. David High and Carol Meadows had one that said Carol Meadows. And I turned to Betty Lou and, um, how do I get a name tag like that? How do I get into, you know, how do you get a name tag with your name on it? And she said, that's how we know you're divorced. And I said, well, I'll hang a little thing from my mind that says still married, because I didn't know what to call people. I mean, I didn't know too, you know? Hello, Mrs. Donnelly, you know, somebody my age, it just seemed weird to me. Yeah, and I can't remember now when it stopped, but it's probably soon after that. Well, I mean, I'll tell you, sometimes it makes hard. Makes it hard to do research on people too, because I feel like I know their name and I can usually find their maiden name in the Junior League directory. We'll have people's maiden names, and sometimes it doesn't occur to me to look up misses and their husband's name, or I don't know their husband's name, so it makes it hard to find people. It does? Yeah. Um, do you remember kind of what the, um, requirements were, I guess for your, did you have a provisional year back then? Yes, we do have a provisional year and I was asked to resign because, uh, the provisional year involved a number of activities and they were all very interesting. But because of my, as I was working full time, there were three of us in my provisional class who were kit Harvey owned her own, uh, daycare program and still does. Carrie Quinn worked for a bank and me and we were the three who worked. And, um. My, my boss was small at the Board of Education was, you know, very lenient. But if there was a state Board of Education meeting on in Pocatello, I had to be there. And so here I was, a former reporter who covered the legislature and was working for the Board of Education. I missed the trip to Boise State. I missed the tour of the legislature, and I missed going to the statesman. I could have given any of those tours myself. And so the woman who was, um, chairman of the membership committee at the time said she that the committee was recommending that I resign because I wasn't showing proper um commitment I guess to, to the Junior League. Unless I could promise that I would not miss any more meetings. And I said something like, well, if one of my kids is hit by a car. She said, I'm not talking about stuff like that. I'm just talking about. And I said I'd try. So, um. So they let me stay in. Years later, when I was being installed as president, she was sitting over at one of the tables. In those days, all the sustainers came to those meetings and they all kind of got drunk and sat around and I almost well, I don't want this would be a terrible thing to have on record, but I came very close to flipping her off because. But they just didn't have a lot of stuff that, um, working women could do in those days. In the evenings. In the evening. So I did a lot of weekend things and things that occurred after work. And, um, and, and things that when I knew I was going to have a difficult year, I'd take something that looked really easy. I think we ought to have easy things in the junior leagues because everybody has different life changes and sometimes you just need to sit for this year. I just don't want to think about very much. How did you decide to. Well, we didn't, we didn't, um, nominate or we didn't do anything like that. I was, um, approached by nominations one year, uh, to be slated as president and I just didn't think it was a good year for me. So I told them no. And then a couple of years later, they called again. And so at that time, I didn't the first time because my son was a senior in high school, and I really thought I was needed here at home during that year. As it turned out, he didn't need me for anything during that year. But um, still, I was nice about it. And then I got the call. And the second time my life was better organized so I could do it at that time. How much more of a commitment did you have to take on to be president? Honestly not much. And part of it was I really used committee chairs to be committee chairs. I did not visit every committee. I know some presidents have done that. I didn't do it. I figured that's what the chairs were for. I had a timer on my phone at work and if people called me, I told them I would only talk for five minutes because I had a job to do. Um. You know, as long as you're kind of organized at the top and you know what people are and you keep in touch with what, what these people are doing and they're in touch with what these people are doing. I didn't have to do that, and at least that's the way I worked it at work. So I didn't think it could be any different in the Junior League. And I don't know if I was well regarded as president or not, but, um, I just didn't spend that much time, I guess, on it. And they'd had a fundraiser the year before I was president. So the Junior League was awash in cash by the time by the time I became president. So I think the woman who did it the year before I did had a lot more to do than I did. Do you feel like by the time you got to be president, that your history as a working person ended up benefiting you in the league? Yes. Although I was not the first working president, I think the woman before me, I think Julie Taylor, was the first, um, working president. She was working at, um, Blue Cross at the time. And so I was no longer an oddity to have a working president. But I think people were respectful of my time. Now it feels so different because I don't I don't know anybody in the league who doesn't work at least part time. Yeah, I know it's it's it's happening everywhere. When I was on the national board, we used to talk about that all the time. Um, you know, women didn't well. For many women who, who were stay at home moms or didn't work, the. Gig was a creative outlet and a lot of needs. To work. Um. The needs of the organization change and the needs of those women change. They didn't need that same kind of thing. They probably wanted more. I'm assuming they wanted friendships and they wanted the team building, and they wanted working women to get on a project, something like that, as opposed to learning how to lead a committee. They come ready to lead a committee now. Do you recall, um, Junior League opening the thrift shop and what kind of. Yes. Uh, commitment that was. Yes I do, it was up on overland in a little house. There was a big deal to do something like this. And, um. My own experience of it was, well, this is one of my experiences there, but it's the one I've remembered the longest. I went to work one day and there was a room that was the room with toys and baby stuff, cribs and things like that. The children's room. And it just didn't smell right. Just something was wrong in that room. So finally I went back and I started moving stuff away from the walls. And somebody had taken a dump on the floor. Okay. Yeah. And so I went into the room with all the kitchen equipment, and I got a spatula and a knife, and I'm down on my hands and knees scrubbing at this or trying to get this poop up off the floor. And a pair of shoes stopped next to me and somebody said, hi, Lindy. And I looked up. It was somebody that I worked with and I said, you know what? You can join the Junior League and spend your Saturdays scraping shit off the floor. But I liked the, um, I liked the surf shop work. It was a change from what I did in my daily life. It was just kind of fun to be there and be really clear about what we were doing. I got a call one day from a from one of our members who was really upset because she had seen one of our members going through and purchasing baby clothes, and she said, we need to leave those there for poor people. And I said, no, we don't. Our our main goal in this operation is to make money, and I'd sell the whole thing to one of our members if I thought we could make enough money on it. You know, we need to be clear, is this a charity or is it a money maker? And for us, it was a money maker. Did they require that you work so many shifts in a week or a month? And I think you could buy your way out of them. Okay. I can't remember if you paid so much per shift or if you got a subsidy. I honestly, I don't remember because I always made my shifts. But I think it was more flexible than. That. Um, were there any other specific Junior League projects that you recall that ended up being important to you, or you feel like you contributed to? Yes. And this was the most this was the one I had most fun with too. We had printed a the cookbook called bound, please. Have you heard the story that marketing grew up on this. I don't think so. Well, you know, there is the bound to. Please copy with the. With the back. The stand up back. Have you seen those? Yes. Okay. Well, it turned out that whoever was on the marketing committee at the time. Had it priced so that. We weren't making anything on them. And the more we sold, the more we lost, because apparently bookstores automatically assume they get 20% off automatically. So when you see the books on sale for 10% off, they're still making. I may have my numbers wrong, but but we were pricing it so that we couldn't sell it to the bookshops because we were losing money on each one that we sold. So we couldn't sell them to the bookshops, we couldn't sell them in mass productions. We couldn't. And that's why there was a second printing, and the second printing was just a hard bound copy. And for the hardbound copy, the decision somebody on the committee made the decision to have each section start with a little thing about Idaho's history or natural resources. I don't know if you've ever even noticed that part, but, um, I'll get it because it was this was really a fun thing to do. This is also where I found myself on the internet one time listed as the author of this book. Um, so here is eggs and cheese and. And the beginning of the section is the diversity of wildlife in the state. So here's this little paragraph on wildlife. And there's one on potatoes. I remember that one. So just a little bit about Idaho. So I so there were three of us or four of us who were assigned to this park and these wonderful women. And honestly, I don't remember who they were. They took all these topics and they just went out and did research on these topics. And then one day after 5:00, they all came down to my office at Health and Welfare, and I set up my word processor and I say, okay, now I need to know about, um, the natural soil. And whoever had that as a topic would go through her file and she'd start telling me things. And then I could write this paragraph out of what she was telling me. And then I'd say, okay, can anybody tell me why the potato is important? Oh, yes, I've got the potato information. And it was just so much fun because they were so good and we got it done really fast. And so you'll see. So the reason I was on the internet is illustrations text by Lindy High. So I was listed on the internet as the author of this book. Okay, good luck with that. Did you contribute any of the recipes? I did, it's the simplest recipe in the whole book. Get some ice cream and a blender. Put some Kahlua on top of it. Quiz it up Up and pour it into a wine glass. All right. Good. Now, I won an award for it. The other one that month. It's a really complicated casserole, and I was laughing at mine. I think we called it a nice name. Basically ice cream and Kahlua. So the junior Junior League of Boise gave awards to people for submitting. Aw, yeah. Every month. You know, some. I don't know how they picked them, but I heard a funny story, too. The recipes were submitted, and then there was the committee that was trying them out. You know, having they'd have like a dinner group over and they'd serve this recipe and then ask people to rate them. And only recipes that got all fives were included in this book. Okay. So Vicki Risch, who's a very nice woman, made her special chicken salad, which apparently was just delicious. And of the four couples there, she got three fives and one fourth that she had rated as a four. And she said why? And he said, well, I didn't want him to think. It was just like I was voting for you. So her recipe didn't get in the book because it got a four. Maybe he wanted to keep it secret. Secret family recipe. Um, transitioning to AJ Lee, which is the international body. What's it called? AJ Lee yeah. Association of Junior Leagues International. When did when did you transition and why did you decide to do that? Um, after I was president, I was elected to a regional office region six. I don't even know if they have regions anymore. And I was the, um. Uh, editor of the region six newsletter that went around to all the leagues in the region. And at the end of that year, I was done. But a woman who had been elected to the National Board of Directors was killed in a car accident. And, um, I believe she's from New Mexico. And so it was somebody from our region and the regional manager suggested me, so I was asked to join. It was elected at the next annual meeting to the board of directors of the association. And I did that for, I think, two years. And the first year I was called director chairing Council. And that meant that this area six Council, I chaired that and then was on the Board of directors representing that council. And then the woman who was vice president of the association had finished one year of a two year term when she was elected on nominations. Nominated her to be president. There was a year left on her term, and I was elected to fill that year as vice president of the association. Basically, I think I had three years at the national level. Did you find at that time that Boise was a unique league in any way, or much like everybody else, or. Well. I would always come back thinking Boise was doing things better. I just thought that Boise really, really had it together and was very creative about a lot of the things it did. And. um, I always felt really good about Boise. I don't think it was particularly unique, but it just seemed to me that Boise ran smoothly and members were interested in what they were doing. I felt good coming from Boise. Um, tell me if I've got this right. In 1981, um, you were secretary of Planned Parenthood in Idaho. Yes. What was going on in the world of family planning at that time? Abortions. Um. Uh, the this was kind of funny. Um. That was at the, at that time was when Planned Parenthood, which had an office in Boise, was opening a second office in Meridian and a health clinic, second health clinic in Meridian. And the men on board wondered what we were doing to advertise it so that, uh, high school kids would know that it was there and that women on the board basically said, well, we'll tell some girls. And they said, yeah, but no, what are you going to do to get the word out? We'll tell some girls. Well, what does that mean? Girls talk about this kind of thing. We don't have to have a PR campaign. They will find out. I just thought that was a nice difference between men and women on the board. I think it's surprising to me that there were men on the board. But I guess. In fact. Some die for you. Yeah. The chairman of the board at that time was a woman named Phoebe Lundy. Who was a professor at Boise State University. We had to go down through the whole thing about why is abortion important? Why offered the service to people who need it and want it, and Phoebe just tired of of. Well, in any organization, when somebody joins, you have to take a step back and kind of bring them along. So that was what was happening. But Phoebe was just tired of it. And she said, I'm so sick of this shit or get off the pot. And I'd been taking notes and I stopped. And father Nate Pearce from from Nampa, he was an Episcopal priest, was sitting next to me and he leaned over and said, in my business, we say, pray or get off your knees. Same meaning though, right? Yeah. Um, all right. Can you tell me about working for the governor's office in 95 through 98? Yes. Great experience. Worked with wonderful people and learned so much about state government. I thought I knew it all before, and I didn't. Um, I had known Phil that uh, when he was in the Senate and I was a reporter covering the Senate. And in fact, when he was elected president pro tem, his competition for that job was Dave's father. They had an election and Dave's dad lost and Phil became president elect. And then later there was a big flap over health and welfare, over a computer system that went awry. And eventually Cecil Andrus appointed Phil Bat to head up a committee to review what happened and make recommendations so that something like that wouldn't happen in the future. Which is pretty funny because he was the least computer oriented person I know. But I stopped that committee as the public information officer for the department. So again, I got to spend some time with him. So. That year in 90 for the 94 elections. The guy I'd been working for at the Department of Education did not run again. And Anne Fox, who was a conservative Republican, was elected and she had her assistant fire me. And he couldn't do it because she hadn't taken office yet. But it was pretty clear I was you know, I was on my way out and somebody, a mutual friend of ours, told feel bad about it. And Phil called me up and said, I want you to come over to the. He had temporary transition offices in the state House. I want you to come over and let me interview you because I'm going to hire you. Well, that is a real easy job interview to go to, and I walked out of that office 15 minutes later with a job and a copy of his inaugural address that he wanted me to edit and get back to him the next day. So that's how I ended up going to the governor's office. Um, I was the. Positive. I was the oldest person on that staff. We had a lot of young in their 20s, fun, fun kids to work with. Um, and the areas that I was assigned were education, health and welfare, a human rights and some minor commissions and committees. Do you recall what are some of the issues facing Idaho during that time? Um, one of the big ones at that time was forming a school of engineering at Boise State University, because there was a lot of resistance from the University of Idaho to doing that. Um, that was the time of, um, a lot of, uh, white Christian nationalism up in North Idaho. White supremacy. Richard Butler at that time. So, um, the Human Rights Commission was having to spend a lot of time doing things like doing things like that. Um, health and welfare was, as always, trying to thread the needle between federal health requirements and what the state would permit. He appointed the woman to be the director who had been head of the welfare division of the Department of Health and Welfare, and she was excellent. And she made my job a lot easier because she believed in just getting things off the table. And if it meant settling a lawsuit, it now, because I don't want to worry about it anymore. And so it was really helpful to have a major Responsibility be handled by somebody as competent as she was. You told me when to stop. You're on the Idaho Barber and cosmetology board. How did you end up doing that? Well, Butch Otter appointed me. Dave and I went to college with Butch, so I've known him for a long time. Uh, the woman who was the head of the Bureau of Occupational Licenses was a woman named Tana. Kori and Tana had been one of my counterparts in the governor's office. And Tana said, you know, we never got to see each other. So when Butch was looking for somebody to fill this position, every licensing board in Idaho needs to have a lay member on it, a public member. And so Tana suggested me for the public member, and it's been a lot of fun. It's something I know nothing about. So I've met some real interesting people and heard some real interesting discussions. I've really enjoyed it. And Idaho is kind of known for not for being not very big on, I guess, regulation, right? So we don't have the same kind of certifying licenses as other states, as I understand sometimes. Like. No, we do. Um, we have standards for people who want cosmetology or barbers. There's different categories, but we set the number of hours of study that you have to have and what the curriculum will be. And, um, then we vet people for, we, we ask about any criminal records. Criminal record doesn't mean he won't be licensed. Um, in fact. I would say every time we meet, there are people who have applied for, um, for a license who explain what their felony was and what they've done to change things since then. And so we we don't hold that against them in a way. If it looks like it's not going to be a problem. Somebody who wants to cut kids hair, who's a registered sex offender is not going to get, um, not going to get it. But, uh, Governor little had all licensing entities go through their, their regulations and basically streamline them. And it was a good exercise because at least in our case, I will say we duplicated a lot of stuff that was already in state code, so we don't have to do that. And it was this kind of writing, uh, a, uh, one big one, then small a then one one, you know, so those things are really hard to read. And it turns out you can write complete sentences and say the same thing. So let's say our rules are streamlined now and a lot easier to use and a lot easier to understand than they were before. Uh, the governor takes a lot of pride in saying we are the least regulated state in the nation. But least regulated doesn't mean no regulation. It just means we spend a lot of stuff. All right. Let me get to my last question, which was, was there anything I should have asked you that I didn't. You know. Um, just one thing. I was also on the ACLU of Idaho board for several years, and you were asking about interesting things that happened while I was on the, um. Planned Parenthood board. The interesting thing that happened on the ACLU board was I was part of the group, filed a lawsuit challenging the state over its, um, public defense system that people were not getting, that ours was not meeting the constitutional requirements of everybody having competent legal advice when they were being tried. And that lawsuit is still going on, and I haven't been on that board for, well, I'd have to look up the dates, but 6 or 7 years, something like that. And that lawsuit is still alive because the state hasn't made it yet. But I like to think that was a good thing for the state. Are you still a part of it in any way, or does the I just. Just a member, member of the ACLU. All right. Anything else I should have asked you? I can't believe you found out, you know. Really? Yeah. Okay. All right. Well, I'm going to review on this and.

lines.line: # Introduction and Oral History Project Overview

> The interviewer introduces herself, the interviewee, and the context of the oral history project, including its sponsors and goals. The purpose of the interview is outlined, focusing on women's lives in Idaho and the history of the Junior League. The interviewee is asked to state and spell her name, setting the stage for the conversation.

metadata.title: Introduction and Oral History Project Overview
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer introduces herself, the interviewee, and the context of the oral history project, including its sponsors and goals. The purpose of the interview is outlined, focusing on women's lives in Idaho and the history of the Junior League. The interviewee is asked to state and spell her name, setting the stage for the conversation.

lines.line: # Family Background and Early Influences

> The speaker discusses her family's deep roots in Idaho, tracing her lineage back five generations. She shares stories about her great-great-grandparents' migration and her mother's remarkable skills and community involvement. Her mother's philosophy of improving the community leaves a lasting impression, shaping the speaker's own values and commitment to service.

metadata.title: Family Background and Early Influences
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses her family's deep roots in Idaho, tracing her lineage back five generations. She shares stories about her great-great-grandparents' migration and her mother's remarkable skills and community involvement. Her mother's philosophy of improving the community leaves a lasting impression, shaping the speaker's own values and commitment to service.

lines.line: # Entering Journalism and Early Career Path

> The speaker describes her transition into journalism after working at the Idaho Historical Society and taking time off to have a child. She recounts how she was recommended for a position at United Press International (UPI) despite not knowing much about wire services. Her honesty about not liking sports ironically helps her secure the job, and she credits her bureau chief with teaching her essential reporting skills that influenced her entire career.

metadata.title: Entering Journalism and Early Career Path
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes her transition into journalism after working at the Idaho Historical Society and taking time off to have a child. She recounts how she was recommended for a position at United Press International (UPI) despite not knowing much about wire services. Her honesty about not liking sports ironically helps her secure the job, and she credits her bureau chief with teaching her essential reporting skills that influenced her entire career.

lines.line: # Wire Services and the Culture of Reporting

> The conversation delves into the differences between Associated Press (AP) and UPI, explaining their structures and funding models. The speaker shares anecdotes about the competitive and sometimes mischievous tactics reporters used to get stories, highlighting the vibrant and aggressive culture of journalism during that era. These experiences provide insight into the practical realities of news gathering before the digital age.

metadata.title: Wire Services and the Culture of Reporting

lines.line: # Early Reporting Assignments and Gender Barriers

> The speaker recalls her baptism by fire in journalism, covering major stories like bank robberies, plane crashes, and killings early in her career. She describes the challenges and learning experiences these assignments provided, including a memorable bank robbery interview. The discussion also touches on the scarcity of women in journalism at the time and the practical difficulties they faced, such as the lack of women's restrooms in the legislature.

metadata.title: Early Reporting Assignments and Gender Barriers
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recalls her baptism by fire in journalism, covering major stories like bank robberies, plane crashes, and killings early in her career. She describes the challenges and learning experiences these assignments provided, including a memorable bank robbery interview. The discussion also touches on the scarcity of women in journalism at the time and the practical difficulties they faced, such as the lack of women's restrooms in the legislature.

lines.line: # Covering the Legislature and the Equal Rights Amendment

> The speaker recounts her experiences covering the Idaho legislature from 1968 to 1972, including the passage and later rescinding of the Equal Rights Amendment (ERA). She describes the initial lack of controversy, the political dynamics, and the subsequent backlash led by conservative groups. The narrative highlights the shifting attitudes toward women's rights and the impact of national movements on local politics.

metadata.title: Covering the Legislature and the Equal Rights Amendment

She describes the initial lack of controversy, the political dynamics, and the subsequent backlash led by conservative groups. lines.line: # Public Recognition and Influence as a Journalist

> The interviewer asks about the speaker's public profile, leading to a discussion of her visibility within state government and later as a newspaper columnist. The speaker notes that her weekly editorial column in the Statesman brought her more recognition than her wire service work. She reflects on the limited influence she felt she had on public policy, despite her prominent role in the media.

metadata.title: Public Recognition and Influence as a Journalist

lines.line: # Reporting on the Idaho State Penitentiary

> The speaker shares her experiences covering incidents at the old Idaho State Penitentiary, including a tense standoff and a murder. She describes the competitive nature of reporting, ethical dilemmas, and the lengths she went to get the story. The narrative includes anecdotes about newsroom rivalries, inside information, and the challenges of reporting on crime and corrections.

metadata.title: Reporting on the Idaho State Penitentiary

lines.line: # Teaching Journalism and the Watergate Influence

> The conversation shifts to the speaker's time teaching journalism at the University of Idaho during the Watergate era. She discusses the impact of Woodward and Bernstein on students' aspirations and her emphasis on mastering the basics of reporting. The speaker describes her practical approach to teaching, her students' ambitions, and her own experiences as both an educator and a learner.

lines.line: # Women's Caucus and Human Rights Advocacy at University of Idaho

> The speaker describes her involvement in the Women's Caucus at the University of Idaho, which filed a complaint with the Human Rights Commission over gender inequality. She details the group's demands, the negotiation process, and the eventual establishment of a Women's Center and other reforms. The speaker reflects on the significance of these changes and the legacy of the Women's Center, which was later closed.

lines.line: # Transition to State Government and Public Information Work

> The speaker discusses her move to the State Board of Education as a public information officer, motivated by the need for full-time work and better pay. She describes the responsibilities of the role, including editing, organizing materials for board meetings, and maintaining transparency with the press. The speaker emphasizes the importance of honesty with reporters and her dual role as both a communicator and occasional stringer for wire services.

lines.line: # Department of Health and Welfare and the Baby Ashley Case

> The speaker recounts her transition to the Department of Health and Welfare, the hiring process, and a notable case involving an abandoned infant known as Baby Ashley. She describes the media attention, the ethical and medical complexities, and the efforts to locate the child's family. The narrative highlights the challenges of public communication in sensitive situations and the emotional impact of unresolved cases.

lines.line: # Joining the Junior League of Boise

> The speaker recalls being invited to join the Junior League of Boise in the late 1970s, describing the semi-secretive recruitment process and her initial unfamiliarity with the organization. She shares anecdotes about social customs, such as name tags indicating marital status, and her early impressions of the league's culture. The narrative provides insight into the organization's traditions and the speaker's adaptation to its norms.

lines.line: # Junior League Membership Requirements and Leadership Journey

> The speaker discusses the requirements for Junior League membership, including the provisional year and the challenges faced by working women. She recounts being asked to resign due to missed meetings, her eventual rise to president, and her approach to leadership. The narrative highlights the evolving expectations for members and the importance of flexibility and understanding in volunteer organizations.

lines.line: # Changing Role of Working Women in the Junior League

> The conversation addresses the increasing presence of working women in the Junior League and the resulting changes in organizational culture. The speaker reflects on how the league adapted to members' professional commitments and the shift from the league as a creative outlet to a space for leadership and teamwork. The discussion underscores the broader societal changes affecting women's volunteer organizations.

lines.line: # Junior League Thrift Shop and Community Projects

> The speaker describes the opening and operation of the Junior League thrift shop, including memorable experiences and the shop's dual role as a fundraiser and community resource. She discusses debates over the shop's purpose, the flexibility of volunteer shifts, and her involvement in other projects like the cookbook. The narrative illustrates the practical and social aspects of league activities.

lines.line: # Involvement with AJLI and National Leadership

> The speaker recounts her progression to regional and national leadership roles within the Association of Junior Leagues International (AJLI). She describes her responsibilities, the circumstances leading to her board appointment, and her impressions of Boise's league compared to others. The speaker expresses pride in Boise's creativity and effectiveness within the broader organization.

lines.line: # Planned Parenthood Board Service and Gender Dynamics

> The speaker discusses her service as secretary of Planned Parenthood in Idaho during the early 1980s, a period marked by debates over abortion and family planning. She highlights the differences in perspective between men and women on the board, particularly regarding outreach strategies. Anecdotes illustrate the challenges of advocacy and the importance of informal communication networks among women.

lines.line: # Working for the Governor's Office and State Issues

> The speaker describes her work in the Idaho governor's office from 1995 to 1998, including her responsibilities in education, health, and human rights. She explains how she was hired, her relationships with political figures, and the major issues facing the state, such as the establishment of an engineering school and responses to white supremacy in North Idaho. The narrative highlights the complexities of state government and the importance of competent leadership.

lines.line: # Idaho Barber and Cosmetology Board Service

> The speaker discusses her appointment as a lay member of the Idaho Barber and Cosmetology Board, recommended by a colleague and appointed by the governor. She describes the board's role in setting licensing standards, reviewing applicants with criminal records, and streamlining regulations. The speaker reflects on Idaho's regulatory philosophy and the practicalities of occupational licensing.

lines.line: # ACLU Board Service and Public Defense Lawsuit

> The speaker mentions her service on the ACLU of Idaho board, highlighting her involvement in a lawsuit challenging the state's public defense system. She explains the ongoing nature of the case and her belief in its importance for ensuring constitutional rights. The speaker concludes by affirming her continued support for the ACLU as a member.

children.text: # Introduction and Oral History Project Overview

> The interviewer introduces herself, the interviewee, and the context of the oral history project, including its sponsors and goals. The purpose of the interview is outlined, focusing on women's lives in Idaho and the history of the Junior League. The interviewee is asked to state and spell her name, setting the stage for the conversation.
# Family Background and Early Influences

> The speaker discusses her family's deep roots in Idaho, tracing her lineage back five generations. She shares stories about her great-great-grandparents' migration and her mother's remarkable skills and community involvement. Her mother's philosophy of improving the community leaves a lasting impression, shaping the speaker's own values and commitment to service.
# Entering Journalism and Early Career Path

> The speaker describes her transition into journalism after working at the Idaho Historical Society and taking time off to have a child. She recounts how she was recommended for a position at United Press International (UPI) despite not knowing much about wire services. Her honesty about not liking sports ironically helps her secure the job, and she credits her bureau chief with teaching her essential reporting skills that influenced her entire career.
# Wire Services and the Culture of Reporting

> The conversation delves into the differences between Associated Press (AP) and UPI, explaining their structures and funding models. The speaker shares anecdotes about the competitive and sometimes mischievous tactics reporters used to get stories, highlighting the vibrant and aggressive culture of journalism during that era. These experiences provide insight into the practical realities of news gathering before the digital age.
# Early Reporting Assignments and Gender Barriers

> The speaker recalls her baptism by fire in journalism, covering major stories like bank robberies, plane crashes, and killings early in her career. She describes the challenges and learning experiences these assignments provided, including a memorable bank robbery interview. The discussion also touches on the scarcity of women in journalism at the time and the practical difficulties they faced, such as the lack of women's restrooms in the legislature.
# Covering the Legislature and the Equal Rights Amendment

> The speaker recounts her experiences covering the Idaho legislature from 1968 to 1972, including the passage and later rescinding of the Equal Rights Amendment (ERA). She describes the initial lack of controversy, the political dynamics, and the subsequent backlash led by conservative groups. The narrative highlights the shifting attitudes toward women's rights and the impact of national movements on local politics.
# Public Recognition and Influence as a Journalist

> The interviewer asks about the speaker's public profile, leading to a discussion of her visibility within state government and later as a newspaper columnist. The speaker notes that her weekly editorial column in the Statesman brought her more recognition than her wire service work. She reflects on the limited influence she felt she had on public policy, despite her prominent role in the media.
# Reporting on the Idaho State Penitentiary

> The speaker shares her experiences covering incidents at the old Idaho State Penitentiary, including a tense standoff and a murder. She describes the competitive nature of reporting, ethical dilemmas, and the lengths she went to get the story. The narrative includes anecdotes about newsroom rivalries, inside information, and the challenges of reporting on crime and corrections.
# Teaching Journalism and the Watergate Influence

> The conversation shifts to the speaker's time teaching journalism at the University of Idaho during the Watergate era. She discusses the impact of Woodward and Bernstein on students' aspirations and her emphasis on mastering the basics of reporting. The speaker describes her practical approach to teaching, her students' ambitions, and her own experiences as both an educator and a learner.
# Women's Caucus and Human Rights Advocacy at University of Idaho

> The speaker describes her involvement in the Women's Caucus at the University of Idaho, which filed a complaint with the Human Rights Commission over gender inequality. She details the group's demands, the negotiation process, and the eventual establishment of a Women's Center and other reforms. The speaker reflects on the significance of these changes and the legacy of the Women's Center, which was later closed.

The speaker emphasizes the importance of honesty with reporters and her dual role as both a communicator and occasional stringer for wire services.

story.title: Interview with Lindy High
story.description: The interview provides insights into the life and career of Lindy High, a fifth-generation Idahoan with a varied career in journalism, state government, and volunteerism, including her significant involvement in the Junior League of Boise. High shares her experiences as a journalist, revealing the challenges faced by women in the field during her time. She discusses her work with the Idaho Historical Society, the Junior League, and her role advocating for women's rights and community service. Additionally, she recounts her experiences with Planned Parenthood, the ACLU, and her tenure in the governor's office, highlighting key societal and political issues in Idaho over several decades.
